section.title: Flux 2-forms
section.p:
The flux formula from is
\iint_S \mathbf F\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS = \iint_D \mathbf F(\mathbf r(u,v))\cdot \bigl(\mathbf r_u\times\mathbf r_v\bigr)\,du\,dv.
This is efficient, but it hides the signed-area pieces. In , the form dx\wedge dy measured oriented area in the xy-plane. A surface in space has three coordinate shadows, so flux needs three oriented area forms: dy\wedge dz,\qquad dz\wedge dx,\qquad dx\wedge dy.

subsection.title: A wall perpendicular to the x-axis
subsection.p:
Take a flat wall x=2,\qquad 0\le y\le3,\qquad 0\le z\le4. Orient it in the positive x-direction. A natural parametrization is \mathbf r(y,z)=(2,y,z). Then
\mathbf r_y=(0,1,0), \qquad \mathbf r_z=(0,0,1),
so \mathbf r_y\times\mathbf r_z=(1,0,0). For a vector field \mathbf F=(P,Q,R), the flux through this wall is
\iint_S \mathbf F\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS \amp= \int_0^3\int_0^4 \mathbf F(2,y,z)\cdot(1,0,0)\,dz\,dy \amp= \int_0^3\int_0^4 P(2,y,z)\,dz\,dy.
Only the x-component of the field crosses a wall whose positive normal points in the x-direction.
The area element on this wall is dy\wedge dz. So the flux integrand is P\,dy\wedge dz.
The other coordinate walls follow the same pattern.
The middle entry dz\wedge dx is not a typo. The ordered directions \mathbf k,\mathbf i have cross product \mathbf k\times\mathbf i=\mathbf j. That is the positive y-normal.

subsection.title: The flux form of a vector field
subsection.p: The coordinate-wall computation suggests the right 2-form.

statement.p:
Let \mathbf F=(P,Q,R) be a vector field in space. The flux 2-form associated to \mathbf F is
\boxed{ \Phi_{\mathbf F} = P\,dy\wedge dz + Q\,dz\wedge dx + R\,dx\wedge dy. }

subsection.p: This form packages the three possible coordinate fluxes:
So the vector field \mathbf F=(P,Q,R) and the 2-form \Phi_{\mathbf F} carry the same flux information.

subsection.title: The shadows of a surface patch
subsection.p:
The formula
P\,dy\wedge dz + Q\,dz\wedge dx + R\,dx\wedge dy
is easier to trust after seeing what it does to one tiny surface patch.

Let
\mathbf a=(a_1,a_2,a_3), \qquad \mathbf b=(b_1,b_2,b_3)
be two tangent vectors spanning a tiny oriented parallelogram. The form dy\wedge dz measures the signed area of the shadow of this parallelogram on the yz-plane:
(dy\wedge dz)(\mathbf a,\mathbf b) = \begin{vmatrix} a_2\amp b_2\\ a_3\amp b_3 \end{vmatrix} = a_2b_3-a_3b_2.
Similarly,
(dz\wedge dx)(\mathbf a,\mathbf b) = a_3b_1-a_1b_3,
and
(dx\wedge dy)(\mathbf a,\mathbf b) = a_1b_2-a_2b_1.
But these three numbers are exactly the components of the cross product:
\boxed{ \mathbf a\times\mathbf b = \bigl( (dy\wedge dz)(\mathbf a,\mathbf b),\, (dz\wedge dx)(\mathbf a,\mathbf b),\, (dx\wedge dy)(\mathbf a,\mathbf b) \bigr). }
Therefore
\Phi_{\mathbf F}(\mathbf a,\mathbf b) \amp= P(dy\wedge dz)(\mathbf a,\mathbf b) + Q(dz\wedge dx)(\mathbf a,\mathbf b) \amp\qquad + R(dx\wedge dy)(\mathbf a,\mathbf b) \amp= (P,Q,R)\cdot(\mathbf a\times\mathbf b) \amp= \mathbf F\cdot(\mathbf a\times\mathbf b).
So the flux 2-form is not a new kind of flux. It is the same flux, written as a signed-area measurement.

subsection.title: Pulling a 2-form back to a parameter domain
subsection.p: Let \mathbf r(u,v)=(x(u,v),y(u,v),z(u,v)) parametrize an oriented surface. To integrate a 2-form over this surface, we rewrite the form in terms of du and dv. This substitution is called a pullback.

The coordinate differentials become dx=x_u\,du+x_v\,dv, dy=y_u\,du+y_v\,dv, and dz=z_u\,du+z_v\,dv. Now compute:
dy\wedge dz \amp= (y_u\,du+y_v\,dv)\wedge(z_u\,du+z_v\,dv) \amp= (y_u z_v-y_v z_u)\,du\wedge dv.
Similarly,
dz\wedge dx = (z_u x_v-z_v x_u)\,du\wedge dv,
and
dx\wedge dy = (x_u y_v-x_v y_u)\,du\wedge dv.

Thus the flux form pulls back to
\boxed{ \begin{aligned} \mathbf r^*\Phi_{\mathbf F} \amp= \Bigl[ P(\mathbf r)(y_u z_v-y_v z_u) + Q(\mathbf r)(z_u x_v-z_v x_u)\\ \amp\qquad + R(\mathbf r)(x_u y_v-x_v y_u) \Bigr]\,du\wedge dv. \end{aligned} }
The coefficient in brackets is exactly
\mathbf F(\mathbf r(u,v))\cdot \bigl(\mathbf r_u\times\mathbf r_v\bigr).

statement.p:
Let S be an oriented surface parametrized by \mathbf r:D\to\mathbb R^3 with the orientation determined by \mathbf r_u\times\mathbf r_v. If \mathbf r^*\Phi=A(u,v)\,du\wedge dv, then
\boxed{ \iint_S \Phi = \iint_D A(u,v)\,du\,dv. }

subsection.p: Reversing the orientation reverses the sign. Covering the same surface twice doubles the integral. These are not defects of the formula; they are reminders that 2-form integrals are integrals over oriented surfaces.

subsection.title: The slanted skylight again
subsection.p:
Return to the skylight
z=1+\frac{x}{2}, \qquad 0\le x\le2,\quad 0\le y\le3,
with upward orientation, and the vector field \mathbf F=(2,1,5). The flux form is
\Phi_{\mathbf F} = 2\,dy\wedge dz + dz\wedge dx + 5\,dx\wedge dy.

Use the parametrization \mathbf r(u,v)=\left(u,v,1+\frac u2\right). Then dx=du,\qquad dy=dv,\qquad dz=\frac12\,du. Pull back each term:
dy\wedge dz = dv\wedge\frac12du = -\frac12\,du\wedge dv,
dz\wedge dx = \frac12du\wedge du = 0,
and
dx\wedge dy = du\wedge dv.
Therefore
\mathbf r^*\Phi_{\mathbf F} \amp= 2\left(-\frac12\,du\wedge dv\right) + 0 + 5\,du\wedge dv \amp= 4\,du\wedge dv.
So
\iint_S \Phi_{\mathbf F} \amp= \int_0^2\int_0^3 4\,dv\,du \amp= 24.
This matches the cross-product computation from .
The form calculation shows where the number 4 came from: 5\,dx\wedge dy gave the upward contribution, while 2\,dy\wedge dz subtracted 1 because the skylight has a yz-shadow with the opposite sign.

subsection.title: Graphs and a useful flux formula
subsection.p:
For a graph z=g(x,y), use the upward orientation and the parametrization \mathbf r(x,y)=(x,y,g(x,y)). Then dz=g_x\,dx+g_y\,dy. Pull back the flux form
\Phi_{\mathbf F} = P\,dy\wedge dz + Q\,dz\wedge dx + R\,dx\wedge dy.
We get
dy\wedge dz = dy\wedge(g_x\,dx+g_y\,dy) = -g_x\,dx\wedge dy,
and
dz\wedge dx = (g_x\,dx+g_y\,dy)\wedge dx = -g_y\,dx\wedge dy.
Therefore
\boxed{ \mathbf r^*\Phi_{\mathbf F} = \bigl(-P g_x-Q g_y+R\bigr)\,dx\wedge dy, }
where P,Q,R are evaluated at (x,y,g(x,y)). So the upward flux through a graph is
\boxed{ \iint_S \mathbf F\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS = \iint_R \bigl(-P(x,y,g)g_x-Q(x,y,g)g_y+R(x,y,g)\bigr)\,dx\,dy. }
This is the same as the cross-product formula, because \mathbf r_x\times\mathbf r_y=(-g_x,-g_y,1).

subsection.title: A cylinder in flux-form language
subsection.p:
Let \mathbf F(x,y,z)=(x,y,0). Then
\Phi_{\mathbf F} = x\,dy\wedge dz + y\,dz\wedge dx.
Parametrize the outward-oriented side of the cylinder of radius a and height h by
\mathbf r(\theta,z)=(a\cos\theta,a\sin\theta,z), \qquad 0\le\theta\le2\pi,\quad 0\le z\le h.
Then
x=a\cos\theta, \qquad y=a\sin\theta,
and
dx=-a\sin\theta\,d\theta, \qquad dy=a\cos\theta\,d\theta.
So
dy\wedge dz = a\cos\theta\,d\theta\wedge dz,
and
dz\wedge dx \amp= dz\wedge(-a\sin\theta\,d\theta) \amp= a\sin\theta\,d\theta\wedge dz.
Pull back the flux form:
\mathbf r^*\Phi_{\mathbf F} \amp= a\cos\theta \bigl(a\cos\theta\,d\theta\wedge dz\bigr) + a\sin\theta \bigl(a\sin\theta\,d\theta\wedge dz\bigr) \amp= a^2(\cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta)\,d\theta\wedge dz \amp= a^2\,d\theta\wedge dz.
Thus
\iint_S \Phi_{\mathbf F} \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^h a^2\,dz\,d\theta \amp= 2\pi a^2h.
Again, this matches the classical flux calculation.

subsection.title: Why the 2-form notation matters
subsection.p: The cross product is fast in \mathbb R^3. The flux 2-form is more revealing.

This form notation also prepares the next theorem. In the plane, Green's theorem said \int_{\partial R}\omega=\int_R d\omega. The derivative of a plane 1-form became a 2-form: d(P\,dx+Q\,dy)=(Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy. In space, the derivative of a 1-form also becomes a 2-form, but now it has three pieces:
d(P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz) \amp= (R_y-Q_z)\,dy\wedge dz \amp\quad+ (P_z-R_x)\,dz\wedge dx \amp\quad+ (Q_x-P_y)\,dx\wedge dy.
Those three coefficients form a vector. That vector is called the curl.

So the next surface integral will look like flux, but it will come from circulation around a boundary curve. A wire loop can bound many different surfaces, and the same boundary integral will know how much curl passes through any one of them.
